1.synthesis: 'And I saw a beast rising out of the sea, with ten horns and seven heads, with ten diadems on its horns and blasphemous names on its heads. And the beast that I saw was like a leopard; its feet were like a bear's, and its mouth was like a lion's mouth. And to it the dragon gave his power and his throne and great authority' (vv.1–2). One of its heads appeared to have a mortal wound but was healed, and the whole earth marveled. Worship is given to the dragon and the beast; the beast is given authority for 42 months to utter blasphemies and make war on the saints. 'Also it was allowed to make war on the saints and to conquer them. And authority was given it over every tribe and people and language and nation, and all who dwell on earth will worship it, everyone whose name has not been written before the foundation of the world in the book of life of the Lamb who was slain' (vv.7–8). 'Here is a call for the endurance and faith of the saints' (v.10).
1.historical_context: The beast rising from the sea deliberately combines all four of Daniel's world-empire beasts (Daniel 7:2–7) into a single figure: the leopard (Greece/Alexander), bear's feet (Persia), lion's mouth (Babylon), ten horns (the fourth beast = Rome). This composite signals that the beast is not one historical empire but the satanic principle of imperial power — the dragon's earthly instrument in every age, embodied in Revelation's own context in the Roman Empire under Domitian. The 'mortal wound that was healed' (v.3) most plausibly refers to the Nero redivivus myth — the popular belief that Nero (who died 68 CE) would return from the dead or from the East at the head of a Parthian army — which the early church applied to Domitian's Nero-like persecution. The 'who is like the beast?' (v.4) parodies the divine name: the question echoes the meaning of 'Michael' (mî kā'ēl = 'who is like God?'), inverting it into blasphemous self-divinization. The Lamb's book of life written 'before the foundation of the world' (v.8) is the counterpoint to the beast's universal authority: the true roster of the redeemed precedes creation itself.
1.literary_structure: The beast from the sea (vv.1–10) is the first member of the satanic trinity that mirrors the divine Trinity: the dragon (corresponding to the Father), the beast from the sea (corresponding to the Son — it receives the dragon's authority as Christ receives the Father's), and the beast from the earth (corresponding to the Spirit — it causes worship of the first beast as the Spirit causes worship of Christ). The counterfeit trinity parodies the divine communion — the dragon gives his power (cf. John 5:26–27, the Father gives the Son authority), the beast blasphemes the divine name, and the false prophet creates a false Pentecost of deception. The beast's universal authority over 'every tribe and people and language and nation' (v.7) directly counters the Lamb's redemption of 'every tribe and language and people and nation' (5:9) — the same universal humanity is claimed by both beast and Lamb, and the book's question is who truly governs it.
1.theological_application: Victorinus of Pettau on the beast: 'This signifies the kingdom of that time of Antichrist, and the people mingled with the variety of nations. His feet were as the feet of a bear — a strong and most unclean beast; the feet are to be understood as his leaders. And his mouth as the mouth of a lion — that is, his mouth armed for blood is his bidding, and a tongue which will proceed to nothing else than to the shedding of blood.' Gill on the book of life (v.8): 'The Lamb's book of life, in which the names of God's elect are written, was written before the foundation of the world; which shows that they were chosen in Christ before that, as the Apostle Paul says, Eph. 1:4; and they were written in it, that is, they were chosen from eternity.' The pastoral call of v.10 — 'Here is a call for the endurance and faith of the saints' — is addressed precisely to communities experiencing the beast's authority. The knowledge that the Lamb's book precedes creation, and that 'if anyone is to be taken captive, to captivity he goes' (v.10) is within the sovereign permission of the enthroned Lamb, grounds the endurance the verse calls for.
1.christology: 'The book of life of the Lamb who was slain' (v.8) — the book of life is identified by its author and custodian: the Lamb who was slain. The election it records is not an abstract decree but a personal commitment grounded in the cross: those written in the book before the foundation of the world are those for whom the Lamb's slaughter was already planned and accomplished. The beast's appearance of overwhelming power — universal worship, conquest of the saints, 42-month authority — is contextualized by this single phrase: there is a prior register, held by the slain Lamb, that overrides the beast's enrollment. Those enrolled in the Lamb's book before creation are not subject to the beast's claim despite its apparent universality. The counterpoint is the book's governing christological assurance: the Lamb's priority is ontological and eternal; the beast's authority is delegated, temporary, and bounded.

11.synthesis: 'Then I saw another beast rising out of the earth. It had two horns like a lamb and it spoke like a dragon. It exercises all the authority of the first beast in its presence, and makes the earth and its inhabitants worship the first beast, whose mortal wound was healed' (vv.11–12). It performs signs (fire from heaven, deluding the world), causes an image of the beast to be made, and gives breath to the image so it can speak and kill those who will not worship it. 'Also it causes all, both small and great, both rich and poor, both free and slave, to be marked on the right hand or the forehead, so that no one can buy or sell unless he has the mark, that is, the name of the beast or the number of its name. This calls for wisdom: let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and his number is 666' (vv.16–18).
11.historical_context: The second beast rising from the earth with 'two horns like a lamb but speaking like a dragon' is later identified as 'the false prophet' (16:13; 19:20; 20:10). The lamb-horns mimic the Lamb of ch.5 — a religious authority that appears sacrificial and innocent but is satanically driven. In the first-century Asian context, the false prophet represents the provincial religious apparatus that enforced imperial cult worship: the priests of the imperial cult in Asia Minor organized festivals, built temples, policed participation, and created the social exclusion the mark entails. Calling down fire from heaven (v.13) parodies Elijah (1 Kings 18:38; 2 Kings 1:10) — the false prophet mimics the true prophetic tradition. The economic exclusion of those without the mark (v.17) reflects the real social situation of Christians who refused to participate in the trade guilds (cf. Thyatira's guild-network) that required ritual acknowledgment of pagan deities. The number 666 — 'the number of a man' (arithmos anthrōpou) — is almost certainly a gematria (Hebrew alphanumeric code) for Nero Caesar (nrwn qsr = 50+200+6+50+100+60+200 = 666 in Hebrew letters).
11.literary_structure: The false prophet completes the satanic trinity. Its role is mimetic and facilitative: it performs signs to validate the beast (as the Spirit performed signs to validate Christ, John 14:11), gives breath to an image (as God breathed life into Adam, Gen 2:7), and causes universal economic participation in the beast-system. The mark on the right hand or forehead (v.16) directly inverts the seal of God on the foreheads of the 144,000 (7:3; 14:1): both marks indicate ownership and allegiance, but the divine seal protects from judgment while the beast's mark brings it. The binary is total: every person either bears the Lamb's name (14:1) or the beast's mark (v.16). The 'wisdom' required to calculate 666 (v.18) is the same wisdom that unlocks the sealed mysteries throughout the book — not esoteric cleverness but the discernment of the Spirit-indwelt community.
11.theological_application: Gill on the lamb-horns and dragon-speech: 'He had two horns like a lamb — he appeared outwardly like a lamb, had a show of religion and piety, pretended to be a minister of Christ, the Lamb of God; but he spoke like a dragon, like the devil, whose temper and spirit he was of, and whose lusts he did.' Barnes on the mark: 'The image seems to be taken from the custom of branding slaves or soldiers, or of marking those who had been initiated into certain mysteries, or who were devotees of a particular deity. This mark was to be either on the right hand or on the forehead — in a conspicuous and public place, so that it would be visible to all.' The economic exclusion of the mark is perhaps the most practically threatening element in Revelation for the communities of Asia Minor: the choice is not theoretical but commercial, social, and daily — buy and sell under the beast's name, or face poverty and social ostracism. The book's answer is the Lamb's book of life (v.8) and the invitation to patient endurance (v.10).
11.christology: The false prophet's two lamb-horns are the most direct christological parody in Revelation: it appears as a Lamb, mimicking the one introduced in ch.5. The false prophet's work — giving breath to an image, performing signs to draw worship — is the satanic parody of the Spirit's work in the church: giving life to dead sinners, performing signs to draw worship to Christ. The 666-number that represents 'the number of a man' stands in contrast to the Lamb's name written on the foreheads of the 144,000 (14:1) — the beast's number represents fallen human sovereignty attempting to claim divine prerogatives, while the Lamb's name represents the divine-human Christ whose sovereignty is genuine. The number falls short of perfection (seven in Revelation's symbolism) at every level — 6, 6, 6 — the triple repetition of incompleteness, the counterpart of the trisagion's triple perfection (Holy, Holy, Holy, 4:8).
